title: Automorphic Forms - Can p-adic integrals be computed?
speaker: Thomas Hales

proof_stages.label: Uniform p-adic definability via quantifier elimination
proof_stages.summary: The talk sets up restricted first-order languages, recalls Tarski, Presburger, Cohen and Pas-style quantifier-elimination results, and emphasizes that uniform definability is the route by which a computer can handle families of local-field problems.

proof_stages.label: Orbital-integral computation as geometry
proof_stages.summary: Stable orbital integrals are introduced as the p-adic integrals of interest, and a low-rank calculation motivates the meaning of 'compute' as extracting rational factors and global geometric objects whose point counts specialize to all local fields.

proof_stages.label: Motivic integration and the Denef-Loeser bridge
proof_stages.summary: Kontsevich-style motivic integration is reviewed through the Grothendieck/Chow-motive target ring, and Denef-Loeser’s theorem is used to connect formulas in the p-adic language to motives whose Frobenius traces recover p-adic volumes.

proof_stages.label: Equal-valuation strips and tubes
proof_stages.summary: For odd orthogonal Lie algebras and their endoscopic partners, regular semisimple elements are restricted to equal-valuation strips and partitioned by reduced characteristic polynomials into tubes on which orbital integrals are conjectured to be constant.

proof_stages.label: Definable transfer factor in the fundamental lemma
proof_stages.summary: A special odd-orthogonal version of the fundamental lemma is stated as an equality of weighted stable orbital integrals, and Waldspurger’s formula implies that the Langlands-Shelstad sign is definable by a very simple formula in the language of rings.

proof_stages.label: Motivic fundamental lemma on tubes
proof_stages.summary: Because the tubes and transfer-factor pieces are definable, Denef-Loeser theory attaches plus and minus virtual Chow motives, yielding a motivic identity whose Frobenius traces recover the classical p-adic fundamental lemma, modulo technical caveats about localization.

proof_stages.label: Algorithmic program and remaining reductions
proof_stages.summary: The conclusion reframes computation of orbital integrals as the search for effective algorithms for the associated virtual motives, with open problems including tube constancy, non-equal-valuation descent and homogeneity, equality of motives, and ultimately the fundamental lemma itself.

examples.name: Quadratic root and discriminant
examples.note: The condition that a quadratic have a real root is used as a toy instance of quantifier elimination, turning an existential statement into the discriminant inequality.
examples.engagement_reason: He applies quantifier elimination to the quadratic-root sentence and identifies the resulting discriminant condition b^2-4ac >= 0.

examples.name: Positive semidefinite quartic
examples.note: The quartic nonnegativity condition illustrates that more complicated semialgebraic questions can still be encoded in Tarski's language and solved by eliminating quantifiers.
examples.engagement_reason: He sets up the positive-semidefinite quartic as a quantified formula and describes running the quantifier-elimination algorithm on that specific quartic form.

examples.name: SO(5) equal-valuation orbital integrals and elliptic curves
examples.note: This low-rank calculation is presented as revealing why computing an orbital integral should mean finding underlying varieties or motives whose finite-field point counts give the local answers.
examples.engagement_reason: He works through the SO(5) equal-valuation setup by forming the reduced characteristic polynomial, defining the associated elliptic curve, and relating the orbital integral to its point count.

passes.reason: The SO(5) equal-valuation calculation is a special but systematic low-rank orbital-integral family producing ordinary elliptic curves, used constructively as a model computation.

The SO(5) example lies in a highly regular equal-valuation strip where all root values have the same valuation, and is used as a model computation.

The SO(5) case is singled out by the highly regular equal-valuation condition on all roots and is used as a positive computed model for orbital integrals.

examples.name: Elementary integral over the p-adic integers
examples.note: The computation of an integral such as the integral of |x|^k over the valuation strata motivates replacing the cardinality q by the Lefschetz class and completing the Grothendieck/motive ring.
examples.engagement_reason: He explicitly computes the p-adic integral over the integers by decomposing by valuation and obtaining a geometric-series expression.
